case_name: Pilot-scale groundwater treatment for MTBE, mercury, and pathogen control before recharge under remote-site constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible pilot-scale treatment process chains for contaminated groundwater intended for groundwater recharge while balancing contaminant removal, cost, operational simplicity, and residuals handling at a remote site.

input_data.scenario_background: A remote industrial property is evaluating a pilot system to treat impacted groundwater prior to managed recharge into an infiltration basin. The plume contains dissolved MTBE from historical fuel releases, low-level dissolved mercury likely associated with site activities, and intermittent microbiological contamination linked to surface intrusion during seasonal well drawdown. Several treatment trains are technically plausible, but the best choice must balance pilot-scale affordability, low operator attention, modest power availability, and avoidance of difficult residuals or concentrate management in a remote location.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should be suitable for controlled groundwater recharge with reliable reduction of MTBE, mercury, and microbial risk, and with stable pilot operation that can inform full-scale selection.
secondary_goals: avoid high-volume hazardous residuals or membrane concentrate
maintain a compact pilot footprint with trailer-mountable equipment
provide resilience to moderate raw water variability

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer minimal solids production and avoid difficult off-site handling of hazardous brines or large spent media volumes
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: system should tolerate intermittent influent variation and short unattended operating periods at a remote location

core_unit_functions.equalization_tank: buffer short-term flow and quality variability for steadier pilot operation
core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: remove fine particulates and protect downstream equipment
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove the bulk of volatile MTBE from groundwater
core_unit_functions.sulfur_impregnated_activated_carbon: polish residual MTBE and adsorb dissolved mercury species

applicability_rationale: MTBE is sufficiently volatile that air stripping can provide major mass removal with relatively simple pilot equipment
using stripping first reduces organic loading on the carbon stage and extends media life
sulfur-impregnated or mercury-selective carbon provides a practical polishing step for low-level dissolved mercury while also handling residual organics
UV is well suited for pathogen control in a low-turbidity groundwater pilot and avoids chemical transport burdens at a remote site
this train balances technical capability with moderate energy use and manageable operator attention
potential_risks: off-gas from air stripping may require vapor-phase carbon depending on local air requirements
mercury adsorption capacity depends on speciation and competing constituents
UV performance can decline if turbidity or iron fouling increases
spent mercury-bearing carbon will require controlled disposal

core_unit_functions.cartridge_prefiltration: protect adsorptive media from solids fouling
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_lead_lag: adsorb MTBE with staged beds to manage breakthrough risk
core_unit_functions.sulfur_polishing_media: capture dissolved mercury remaining after primary adsorption
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: inactivate V. cholerae prior to recharge

applicability_rationale: all-adsorptive treatment with UV avoids stripping towers and may be simpler to permit at a remote pilot location
lead-lag carbon operation improves monitoring and replacement planning for MTBE breakthrough
a dedicated mercury-polishing bed better addresses the fact that conventional GAC is not the strongest stand-alone option for mercury
the train is compact and trailer-friendly
potential_risks: MTBE can have weaker adsorption on GAC than heavier hydrocarbons, increasing media replacement frequency
dual media replacement logistics may be challenging in remote areas
performance is sensitive to empty bed contact time and influent DOC
mercury-bearing spent media requires careful disposal

core_unit_functions.oxidation_for_mercury_precipitation: convert dissolved mercury to a more removable particulate-associated form
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: aggregate precipitated mercury and suspended solids
core_unit_functions.bag_filtration: separate generated solids before downstream treatment
core_unit_functions.chlorination_contact: provide disinfectant residual and inactivate V. cholerae

applicability_rationale: this route is technically capable and uses established process steps for mixed contaminant control
chemical precipitation can be effective for mercury when dissolved speciation is favorable
air stripping remains an efficient primary MTBE removal step
potential_risks: chemical dosing control is harder to maintain during unattended pilot operation
generated mercury-bearing sludge creates a hazardous residual management burden
chlorine delivery and storage are less desirable at a remote location
overdosing coagulants can increase solids handling and upset downstream units

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: physically reject dissolved mercury species and many microorganisms while reducing dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide pathogen barrier redundancy before recharge
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry for recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: membranes can be technically strong for dissolved metals and microbial barriers
this route may be considered when broader dissolved constituent reduction is also desired
compact skid-mounted systems can be piloted
potential_risks: MTBE rejection by membranes is not the most efficient or economical primary control strategy compared with volatilization treatment
concentrate management is a major drawback for a remote pilot site
membrane fouling, cleaning, and instrumentation needs increase operator burden
post-treatment stabilization adds complexity

core_unit_functions.adsorptive_mercury_media: capture dissolved mercury after oxidation step

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade MTBE and is technically plausible at pilot scale
a dedicated mercury sorbent can address the metal fraction
potential_risks: AOP systems for MTBE often require careful oxidant dosing, contact control, and monitoring that are difficult at remote sites
incomplete oxidation can form intermediates and complicate performance interpretation
chemical supply and safety management reduce suitability for a low-attention pilot
UV used for AOP service and disinfection can increase lamp maintenance demands

preferred_rank_pattern: air_stripping_based_routes should rank near the top because MTBE is the dominant mass contaminant and volatile
adsorptive_routes should appear as plausible alternatives, especially when paired with dedicated mercury polishing and UV
membrane_or_AOP_options should not rank first under pilot-only, capital-constrained, remote-site conditions
at least one technically capable option should rank lower because residuals handling or operational complexity is misaligned with the scenario

must_reflect: pilot-only scale favors modular, easy-to-monitor process trains
capital constraint penalizes membrane-heavy or chemically intensive systems
remote location favors lower chemical dependency, simpler O&M, and fewer hazardous residual streams

must_contain: why MTBE volatility makes air stripping attractive for groundwater
why mercury often needs a dedicated polishing or precipitation step rather than assuming MTBE treatment units will remove it
why pathogen control still requires a discrete disinfection barrier prior to recharge
why concentrate or hazardous sludge management downgrades some technically feasible options

should_mention: MTBE breakthrough or incomplete removal risk
mercury speciation uncertainty and media disposal issues
remote-site reliability and unattended operation concerns
